screen.name: screen1
screen.instruct:
The first step is to dilute the blood with a diluting solution.
Use the pipette to add a certain amount of diluting solution to both empty containers, called cuvettes.
screen.label: Sample
Control
Diluting Solution

screen.name: screen2
screen.instruct:
Good. Now add a small amount of blood to the cuvette labeled “sample”.
The other cuvette, with only the diluting solution, will be our control.
screen.label: Sample
Control

screen.name: screen3
screen.instruct: The chemicals in the diluting solution remove the oxygen from the oxyhemoglobin, but they do not affect the carboxyhemoglobin.
screen.caption: Sample
Deoxyhemoglobin (deoxyHb)
Oxyhemoglobin (O2Hb)
Carboxyhemoglobin (COHb)

screen.name: screen4
screen.instruct: As a result, we’ll end up having only two molecules in our sample: deoxyhemoglobin and carboxyhemoglobin.
screen.caption: Sample
Deoxyhemoglobin (deoxyHb)
Oxyhemoglobin (O2Hb)
Carboxyhemoglobin (COHb)

screen.name: screen5
screen.instruct:
This is our machine, a spectrophotometer.
Let me explain how it works while we’re waiting for it to calibrate.
screen.label: Sample
Control
Calibrating...

screen.name: screen6
screen.instruct:
Inside the spectrophotometer is a light source, which shoots a beam of light of a certain wavelength on the sample.
This sample contains two different molecules.
screen.caption: Light Source
Light
High intensity
Sample
Molecule A
Molecule B
Detector

screen.name: screen7
screen.instruct:
A detector measures the intensity of the light that leaves the sample.
Since the molecules in the sample ABSORB some of the light, the light that leaves the sample is less intense than the light that went into the sample.
screen.caption: Light Source
Light
High intensity
Sample
Light
Lower Intensity
Detector

screen.name: screen8
screen.instruct:
Now let’s look at two different samples.
Click on both light sources to see what happens when the light hits the samples.
screen.caption: Sample 1
Sample 2
Light Source
Detector

screen.name: screen9
screen.instruct: Which of the two samples absorbed more light?
screen.caption: Sample 1
Sample 2
Light Source
Detector
screen.wrongText: Remember, if more light is absorbed by the sample, less of the light leaves the sample. Try again.
screen.rightText: Yes– the light that leaves sample 2 is less intense than the light that leaves sample 1, meaning more of it has been absorbed.

screen.name: screen10
screen.instruct: Why do you think sample 2 absorbs more light than sample 1?
screen.caption: Sample 1
Sample 2
Light Source
Detector
screen.option: The width of the cuvettes is different
The concentration of molecules is different
screen.wrongText: No, both cuvettes have the same width. But there’s something different inside the cuvettes. Try again.
screen.rightText:
Good observation. Although there are the same types of molecules in each cuvette, the concentration is different.
The ABSORBANCE of a sample (the extent to which light is absorbed) depends on the concentrations of the different molecules in the sample.

screen.name: screen11
screen.instruct:
Before we measure the blood sample, we'll measure the diluting solution alone.
Place the control cuvette in the spectrophotometer and click the start button.
screen.label: Sample
Control

screen.name: screen12
screen.instruct:
The machine is set up in a way that it will zero out the measured absorbance of the diluting solution.
This way we can be sure that when we measure the sample, it will only display the absorbance of the carboxyhemoglobin.
screen.label: Sample
Control
Measuring...

screen.name: screen13
screen.instruct:
Now, place the control back in the cuvette holder and place the cuvette with the blood sample in the machine.
Click the start button when you’re done.
screen.label: Sample
Control

screen.name: screen14
screen.instruct: The machine will automatically calculate the amount of carboxyhemoglobin in the sample based on the absorbance.
screen.label: Sample
Control
Measuring...
